effective-stress.title: Effective Stress
effective-stress.theory: Effective stress is the portion of total stress carried by the soil skeleton — not the pore water. It controls ALL mechanical behavior: shear strength, consolidation, and liquefaction resistance.
effective-stress.explanation: When soil is loaded, pore water initially carries the load (excess pore pressure). As water drains, stress transfers to the soil skeleton (effective stress increases). This is consolidation.
effective-stress.example: A clay layer at 5m depth with water table at surface: σ = 18×5 = 90 kPa, u = 10×5 = 50 kPa, σ' = 40 kPa. After drainage: σ' increases → strength increases.

shear-strength.title: Shear Strength
shear-strength.theory: The maximum shear stress a soil can withstand before failure. Governed by Mohr-Coulomb criterion. Depends on effective stress (drained) or total stress (undrained).
shear-strength.explanation: c' = effective cohesion (kPa) — true bonding between particles. φ' = friction angle (°) — depends on particle shape, mineralogy. Sand: c'≈0, φ'=28-42°. Clay: c'>0, φ'=20-30°.
shear-strength.example: Slope failure in clay: τ along failure surface reaches τf. Stability number Ns = cu/γH. For rotational slip: Factor of Safety = ΣResisting moments / ΣDriving moments.

consolidation.title: Consolidation
consolidation.theory: Time-dependent compression of saturated fine-grained soils as excess pore water pressure dissipates. Terzaghi 1D theory applies to normally consolidated clays.
consolidation.explanation: Tv = time factor (dimensionless). cv = coefficient of consolidation (m²/year). H = drainage path (half layer for double drainage). U = degree of consolidation.
consolidation.example: 5m clay layer, cv = 2 m²/year, double drainage. Time for 90% consolidation: T90 = 0.848, t = 0.848×(2.5)²/2 = 2.65 years.

liquefaction.title: Liquefaction
liquefaction.theory: Saturated loose sandy soils lose effective stress (and thus strength) during cyclic earthquake loading. Excess pore pressure builds up until u = σ, causing σ' = 0 — soil behaves like a liquid.
liquefaction.explanation: FL < 1.0: Liquefiable. FL = 1.0–1.5: Marginal. FL > 1.5: Safe. Critical for Japan — the 1964 Niigata earthquake, 1995 Kobe, and 2011 Tohoku all caused massive liquefaction.
liquefaction.example: Niigata (1964): Showa Bridge collapsed due to liquefaction of loose river sand deposits. amax=0.16g, N<10 sands with water table at 1m depth → σ'v very small → FL<1 in most areas.

p-wave.title: P-Wave (Primary / Compressional)
p-wave.theory: P-waves are compressional body waves. Particle motion is parallel to propagation direction (push-pull). Fastest seismic wave. Can travel through solids AND liquids/gases.
p-wave.explanation: Typical Vp values: Air 340 m/s. Water 1500 m/s. Loose sand 200-800 m/s. Dense sand 800-2000 m/s. Rock 3000-6000 m/s. Saturated soil: Vp dominated by water (≈1500 m/s).
p-wave.example: Seismic refraction survey: Layer 1 Vp1=400 m/s (loose fill), Layer 2 Vp2=1600 m/s (saturated sand), Layer 3 Vp3=4000 m/s (bedrock). Crossover distances determine layer depths.

s-wave.title: S-Wave (Shear / Secondary)
s-wave.theory: S-waves are shear body waves. Particle motion is perpendicular to propagation direction. Cannot travel through liquids. Fundamental for site response analysis and Vs30 calculation.
s-wave.explanation: Vs is the KEY parameter for geotechnical site characterization. Measured by PS logging, MASW, seismic CPT, crosshole. Vs30 = average Vs in top 30m, used for Japanese seismic design code site classification.
s-wave.example: Soft clay: Vs = 80-150 m/s. Dense sand: Vs = 200-400 m/s. Soft rock: Vs = 400-700 m/s. Hard rock: Vs > 700 m/s. Site Class D (告示): Vs30 < 150 m/s → high amplification.

spt.title: Standard Penetration Test (SPT)
spt.theory: The SPT uses a 63.5 kg hammer falling 760 mm to drive a split-spoon sampler 150+300 mm. The blow count for the last 300 mm = N-value. Most common soil investigation in Japan.
spt.explanation: N-value interpretation: N<4: very loose/soft. N=4-10: loose. N=10-30: medium dense. N=30-50: dense. N>50: very dense/rock. Japanese practice uses rod energy ratio ER ≈ 72% (automatic hammer).

cpt.title: CPTu (Cone Penetration Test with Pore Pressure)
cpt.theory: CPTu continuously measures tip resistance (qc), sleeve friction (fs), and pore pressure (u2) as a cone penetrates at 20 mm/s. Provides continuous soil profile without disturbance.
cpt.explanation: Ic < 1.31: Gravelly sand. 1.31-2.05: Clean sand. 2.05-2.60: Silty sand. 2.60-2.95: Sandy silt. 2.95-3.60: Silty clay. >3.60: Clay. Pore pressure Bq = (u2-u0)/(qt-σv).
cpt.example: At 10m: qc=5 MPa, fs=80 kPa, u2=120 kPa. σv=180 kPa, σ'v=100 kPa, u0=80 kPa. qt=5.03 MPa. Qt=48.5. Fr=1.68%. Ic=2.11 → Sandy silt/silty sand boundary.

ps-logging.title: PS Logging (Downhole Seismic)
ps-logging.theory: PS logging measures P and S wave travel times in a borehole using a downhole receiver and surface source. Provides accurate velocity profile for Vs30 and dynamic property calculation.
ps-logging.explanation: Downhole: single receiver moved down borehole, source at surface offset 1-2m. Interval method: use first arrival times at each depth. Crosshole: source and receiver in separate boreholes — better for soft layers.
ps-logging.example: 10m depth: Δt_P = 14 ms, Δt_S = 32 ms for 1m interval. Vp = 1/0.014 = 71 m/s? No — typical readings: arrival at 10m vs 11m: ΔtP=2ms → Vp=500 m/s. ΔtS=5ms → Vs=200 m/s. G0 = 1800×200² = 72 MPa.

seismic-refraction.title: Seismic Refraction Survey
seismic-refraction.theory: Surface seismic method that uses first-arrival travel times to map subsurface velocity layers. Waves refract (bend) at interfaces where velocity increases with depth — Snell's Law.
seismic-refraction.explanation: Geophone array (24-48 channels). Source: sledgehammer or seismic gun. V1 < V2 required (velocity increase with depth). Cannot detect velocity inversions (hidden layers). Used to map bedrock depth, water table, fill thickness.
seismic-refraction.example: Survey result: V1=400 m/s (fill), V2=1600 m/s (saturated sand), V3=4000 m/s (bedrock). Crossover distances: xc12 = 2h1√((V2-V1)/(V2+V1)). Bedrock depth = 12m. Used for tunnel route assessment.

resistivity.title: Electrical Resistivity Survey
resistivity.theory: Measures bulk electrical resistivity of the ground to map geological structures. Four-electrode arrays: Wenner, Schlumberger, dipole-dipole. Injects current (AB), measures voltage (MN).
resistivity.explanation: Typical resistivities: Sea water 0.2 Ωm. Saturated clay 1-20 Ωm. Saturated sand 20-200 Ωm. Dry sand 500-10000 Ωm. Granite 1000-10000 Ωm. Used for: saltwater intrusion, buried waste, fault detection, groundwater.
resistivity.example: Coastal site: 2D resistivity section shows zone ρ<5 Ωm at 10-20m depth → saltwater intrusion in sand aquifer. Used to define freshwater/saltwater interface for well placement.

masw.title: MASW Surface Wave Survey
masw.jp: 表面波探査・微動探査
masw.theory: MASW (Multichannel Analysis of Surface Waves) measures Rayleigh wave dispersion to invert for Vs profile. Microtremor H/V ratio gives site fundamental frequency. Both methods are non-invasive — no borehole required.
masw.explanation: MASW: 24-channel array, 4.5 Hz geophones, 2-5 m spacing. Sledgehammer source at one end. Dispersion curve extracted from f-k analysis → inversion → Vs(z) profile. Microtremor uses ambient noise recorded with 3-component seismometer — no active source needed.
masw.example: H/V survey at Tokyo soft ground: f0 = 1.0 Hz → site period T = 1.0 s → thick soft deposits (~40m). H/V amplitude > 4 → strong seismic amplification expected. MASW confirms Vs30 = 140 m/s → 第3種地盤 classification.

borehole-camera.title: Borehole Camera Investigation
borehole-camera.theory: A borehole camera (孔内カメラ) is a waterproof optical probe lowered into a borehole to directly observe the borehole wall. It provides visual images or video of rock and soil features that cannot be captured by core recovery alone. Used in both soil and rock investigations.
borehole-camera.explanation: Modern borehole cameras provide 360° panoramic images at any depth. They reveal fracture orientation, aperture (crack width), weathering condition, joint infill material, and groundwater inflow locations. Camera data is essential for confirming what the core log shows — and detecting features lost during drilling.
borehole-camera.example: Tunnel project in Hokkaido granite. Borehole camera at 30–50m depth shows steeply dipping fractures (dip 70°) oriented toward planned tunnel face. Camera reveals open joints with water inflow at 42m. Design changed: increased shotcrete thickness + additional rock bolts. Without camera, this risk would have been missed.

pressuremeter.title: Pressuremeter Test (PMT)
pressuremeter.theory: The pressuremeter test inflates a cylindrical probe inside a borehole to measure the in-situ lateral stress-strain relationship of the soil or rock. It directly measures horizontal deformation behavior — something no other standard test provides. Based on the Ménard pressuremeter (1956).
pressuremeter.explanation: The probe is inserted into a pre-drilled borehole at the test depth. Inflation pressure is increased in steps while measuring volume change. Three phases: (1) borehole wall reopening, (2) pseudo-elastic expansion, (3) plastic zone growth. Em is taken from the linear elastic phase. PL is the pressure at which soil fails (limit pressure).
pressuremeter.example: Soft rock tunnel portal in Kyushu. Pressuremeter tests at 3m, 5m, 8m depths. Results: Em = 15, 22, 35 MPa. PL = 0.8, 1.2, 2.1 MPa. Foundation design uses Em/3 ≈ 5-12 MPa as working modulus. Pressuremeter data more reliable than lab testing for weathered rock because sample disturbance is minimized.

rqd.title: Rock Quality Designation (RQD)
rqd.theory: RQD (Rock Quality Designation) quantifies the degree of jointing or fracturing in a rock mass. It is measured from drill core as the percentage of intact core pieces longer than 100mm in a given drill run. Developed by Deere (1963) and now the standard for rock mass classification worldwide.
rqd.explanation: Only natural fractures are counted — mechanical breaks from drilling are excluded. RQD rating: 90-100% Excellent (優良), 75-90% Good (良), 50-75% Fair (普通), 25-50% Poor (不良), <25% Very Poor (極めて不良). RQD feeds into RMR (Bieniawski 1989) and the NGI Q-system for tunnel support design.
rqd.example: Core run in granite tunnel site (Osaka): 2.0m run. Intact pieces (natural breaks only): 8cm, 25cm, 35cm, 42cm, 32cm, 28cm, 15cm = sum 7 pieces. Pieces ≥ 100mm: 25, 35, 42, 32, 28 cm = 162cm. RQD = 162/200 × 100% = 81% → GOOD. Tunnel support: spot bolts + mesh. No heavy steel sets needed.

plate-load-test.title: Plate Load Test
plate-load-test.theory: The plate load test directly measures the bearing capacity and deformation modulus of soil or rock at the foundation level. A rigid steel plate (typically 300mm or 750mm diameter) is loaded in increments while measuring settlement. It is the most direct measurement of ground response under real foundation conditions.
plate-load-test.explanation: The test is performed at the design foundation level in a pit or directly on the ground surface. A hydraulic jack applies load through the steel plate onto the soil. Settlement is measured with dial gauges or LVDTs. Load-settlement curve defines: elastic zone, yield point, ultimate bearing capacity. Japanese road design uses K30 as the primary subgrade parameter.
plate-load-test.example: Road subgrade test in Saitama. 300mm plate on compacted fill. Load applied in 5 steps: 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 kPa. Settlement at 250 kPa: 12mm. K30 = (250 kPa at s=1.25mm): K30 = p(at 1.25mm)/1.25mm × 1000 = 120 MN/m³. Japanese specification: K30 ≥ 110 MN/m³ for road base → PASS. E0 = (1-0.3²)×0.785×p×0.3/s = ~65 MPa.

gpr.title: Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)
gpr.theory: Ground Penetrating Radar emits short pulses of electromagnetic energy into the ground and records the reflections from subsurface interfaces. The reflection time (two-way travel time) and wave velocity allow calculation of depth to reflectors. GPR is the primary non-invasive method for detecting shallow features: buried utilities, voids, rebar, and geological boundaries.
gpr.explanation: GPR antennas range from 25 MHz (deep penetration, low resolution) to 2 GHz (high resolution, shallow). In Japan, 200-500 MHz antennas are most common for geotechnical work. GPR works best in dry, resistive soils. Wet clay and seawater-saturated ground severely attenuate the signal. Output is a radargram showing reflection hyperbolas from point targets (pipes, rebars) and continuous reflectors (layer boundaries).
gpr.example: Pre-construction utility survey in Tokyo. 500 MHz antenna survey reveals: water pipe at 0.8m depth (hyperbolic reflection), old gas line at 1.2m (metallic reflection, strong), unknown void at 2.1m depth (bright reflection from top, no signal below). GPR data allows safe excavation planning. Also used for bridge deck rebar detection and tunnel lining inspection in Japan.
